Algeria.text: Algeria scraped through the group stage on the strength of a frantic 3-3 draw with Austria — Mahrez's injury-time equaliser cancelling out Kalajdzic's 96th-minute gut-punch, a match that swung violently in both directions and ultimately flattered no one. They advance as a best third-placed side, which tells its own story about the unevenness of their tournament so far. Switzerland await in the Round of 32 on Thu Jul 2 20:00 PT, a compact and disciplined European side who will fancy their chances against an Algerian defence that has already shipped seven goals. Survive that, and Colombia or Ghana could follow — before the draw opens up ominously, with Argentina lurking as a potential quarterfinal opponent.

Argentina.text: Argentina swept through Group J without dropping a point, and their 3-1 dismissal of Jordan was as straightforward as the scoreline suggests — two goals before half-time, a Jordan equaliser that barely interrupted proceedings, and Messi adding a third with ten minutes to go to settle any residual nerves. Cape Verde await in the Round of 32 on Fri Jul 3 15:00 PT, a tie the defending champions are expected to navigate without great difficulty, though the Islanders are no strangers to upsetting larger nations. Survive that, and Australia or Egypt loom in the last 16, before a potential quarterfinal against Colombia or Algeria — and beyond that, the draw offers the genuine prospect of a semifinal collision with Brazil, England, or Mexico.

Australia.text: A goalless draw with Paraguay to close the group stage — cautious, attritional, and ultimately enough to see Australia through as runners-up in Group D. They qualified, but the performance offered little to frighten anyone. Egypt await in the Round of 32 on Fri Jul 3 11:00 PT, a genuinely open contest between two sides of comparable standing; neither carries the weight of favouritism, and a tense, low-scoring affair seems the likeliest shape. Survive that, and Argentina could loom in the Round of 16 — a rather more formidable proposition, though Cape Verde represent the alternative path to a quarterfinal where Colombia or Switzerland might lie in wait.

Austria.text: Austria scraped through Group J in the most nerve-shredding fashion imaginable: a 3-3 draw with Algeria that swung twice in the final minutes, Mahrez equalising at 90+3' before Kalajdzic rescued a point — and Austria's knockout place — three minutes later in stoppage time. That kind of cardiac football will not go unnoticed by Spain, who await in the Round of 32 on Thu Jul 2 12:00 PT; this is a formidable assignment, and Austria enter it as clear underdogs against one of the tournament's genuine contenders. Survive that, and Croatia or Portugal loom in the Round of 16 — with Belgium a potential quarterfinal opponent beyond them.

Belgium.text: Belgium closed out Group G in the manner of a side that had been saving something: Trossard helped himself to a brace before the hour, Bruyne added a third, and late goals from Lukaku and Saelemaekers turned a comfortable win into a rout, the 5-1 scoreline against New Zealand the most emphatic statement of their tournament so far. Senegal await in the Round of 32 on Wed Jul 1 13:00 PT — a genuinely testing assignment, given the Africans' athleticism and tactical discipline, though Belgium's depth and individual quality make them clear favourites. Should they advance, Bosnia & Herzegovina or the United States loom in the last 16, with a potential quarterfinal collision against Portugal or Spain waiting to separate the contenders from the pretenders.

Bosnia & Herzegovina.text: Bosnia & Herzegovina closed their group campaign with something approaching authority — two quick goals before half-time against Qatar, and a third from Mahmic in the 80th minute to make the scoreline comfortable despite Al-Haydos briefly unsettling the lead just before the break. They advance as one of the best third-placed teams, which makes the Round of 32 assignment blunt: the host nation USA await on Wed Jul 1 17:00 PT, and for all Bosnia's finishing form, Gregg Berhalter's side carry home-crowd momentum and considerable expectation — this is a genuine test, not a routine assignment. Survive that, and Belgium or Senegal could follow, with Portugal or Spain potentially lurking in the quarterfinal.

Brazil.text: Brazil came through their Round of 32 tie against Japan the hard way — trailing to Sano's opener before Casemiro levelled after the break, then needing a Martinelli intervention deep in stoppage time to seal a 2-1 win that was more uncomfortable than a team of their stature would care for. Norway arrive for the Round of 16 on Sun Jul 5 13:00 PT as a stern but surmountable test: physically formidable and well-organised, they are capable of frustrating anyone, yet Brazil enter as clear favourites and will expect to impose themselves once the game opens up. Survive that, and England or Mexico could await in the quarterfinal, with Argentina a distinct possibility lurking in the semifinal draw.

Canada.text: Canada survived a nerve-shredding Round of 32 against South Africa, Stephen Eustáquio's goal deep into stoppage time the only thing separating the sides after ninety minutes of stubborn resistance from the Africans. The hosts now face Morocco in the Round of 16 on Sat Jul 4 10:00 PT — a genuine test against a side that reached the World Cup semifinals three years ago, and one that will demand considerably more from Canada than a single late intervention. Win that, and France, Paraguay, or Sweden could await in the quarterfinal on Thu Jul 9 13:00 PT, with the prospect of Spain, Portugal, or the USA looming beyond if Canada's run extends to the last four.

Cape Verde.text: Cape Verde ground out three successive draws in Group H — the goalless stalemate with Saudi Arabia the tidiest summary of a group stage defined by stubborn defending and collective industry rather than invention. They qualified, but no one could accuse them of dazzling their way through. What awaits them in the Round of 32 on Fri Jul 3 15:00 PT is the starkest possible examination: Argentina, world champions and tournament heavyweights, against whom Cape Verde will be decided underdogs asked to conjure something from almost nothing. Survive that, and Australia or Egypt represent a more negotiable path, with Colombia or Switzerland potentially looming in the quarterfinal.

Colombia.text: Colombia finished top of Group K with something close to a statement: a goalless draw against Portugal in their final group game, a result that suited the Colombians well enough and underlined their defensive solidity across the tournament. They meet Ghana in the Round of 32 on Fri Jul 3 18:30 PT, a fixture that, on reputation and recent form, Colombia should approach as heavy favourites — though African sides at this stage of a World Cup rarely concede the tie without a fight. Survive that, and Algeria or Switzerland await in the Round of 16; deeper still, Argentina loom as a potentially explosive quarterfinal possibility on Sat Jul 11 18:00 PT.

Croatia.text: Croatia edged past Ghana 2-1 in a match that followed a familiar, nerve-shredding script — Sucic's first-half opener was cancelled out late by Luckassen, before Vlasic rescued the points with seven minutes remaining. It was hardly commanding, but it was enough, and Croatia's talent for grinding out results at tournaments should not be underestimated. They meet Portugal in the Round of 32 on Thu Jul 2 16:00 PT, a formidable assignment against one of the competition's genuine heavyweights — Cristiano Ronaldo's side will enter as clear favourites, making this a significant test of Croatia's knockout-tournament mettle. Survive that, and Spain could await in the Round of 16, with France a possibility lurking in the semifinals.

Curaçao.text: Curaçao closed their group stage campaign with a 0-2 defeat to Ivory Coast, Nicolas Pépé striking early and then again just past the hour to make it a controlled, comfortable evening for the Elephants. The result confirmed what the tournament had largely borne out across three matches: Curaçao, plucky debutants on the greatest stage, were outgunned at this level. They exit with a single point, a goal difference of minus nine, and the distinction of having competed — if not quite threatened — against top-tier opposition in Group E.

Czech Republic.text: Czech Republic's group stage exit arrived with little ceremony — a 0-3 defeat to Mexico that was effectively settled inside the opening quarter-hour of the second half, Chávez and Quiñones striking within six minutes of each other before Fidalgo's stoppage-time finish applied a flattering coat of finality to what had already been a comfortable Mexican evening. The Czechs finished fourth in Group A with a single point and a goals difference of minus four, scraping through as one of the better third-placed sides before their tournament ended here, emphatically, on the wrong side of a mismatch they could never quite solve.

DR Congo.text: DR Congo finished third in Group K but left no doubt about their quality against Uzbekistan, coming from behind to win 3-1 in a performance that gathered force as it went — Wissa's penalty broke the resistance midway through the second half, Mayele extended the lead, and Wissa wrapped it with a stoppage-time finish that flattered neither the scoreline nor the manner of it. England await in the Round of 32 on Wed Jul 1 09:00 PT, a daunting proposition by any measure: Gareth Southgate's side carry the weight of expectation and the resources to justify it, and Congo enter as clear underdogs. Survive that, and either Ecuador or Mexico could follow — with Brazil or Norway lurking at the quarterfinal stage to make the mountain steeper still.

Ecuador.text: Ecuador finished their group stage with a statement — going a goal down inside two minutes against Germany, they recovered to win 2-1, Angulo equalising almost immediately before Plata sealed it late to confirm their passage as one of the best third-placed sides. They meet Mexico in the Round of 32 on Tue Jun 30 18:00 PT, a derby charged with history in which neither side can claim comfortable favouritism, though Ecuador's momentum against top opposition gives them every reason for confidence. Survive that, and England or DR Congo await in the last 16, with Brazil or Norway potentially looming in the quarterfinal.

Egypt.text: Egypt's group stage ended on an uncertain note — Saber struck inside five minutes against Iran, but the lead lasted less than ten before Rezaeian levelled, and the Pharaohs had to settle for a draw that confirmed second place rather than top spot. They head into the Round of 32 against Australia on Fri Jul 3 11:00 PT as moderate favourites, though the Socceroos are a well-organised side capable of making life uncomfortable, and Egypt's inability to hold a lead will invite pressure. Survive that, and Argentina could await in the Round of 16 — a fixture that would test this team's ambitions in the sharpest possible terms.

England.text: England ground out a professional group-stage finish against Panama, a brace inside five second-half minutes from Bellingham and Kane wrapping up a controlled 2-0 win that flattered neither side with drama. They topped Group L and face DR Congo in the Round of 32 on Wed Jul 1 09:00 PT — a tie England enter as firm favourites, though Congo's physical directness will demand concentration rather than complacency. Survive that, and Ecuador or Mexico await in the Round of 16, before a potential quarterfinal collision on Sat Jul 11 14:00 PT with Brazil or Norway that would represent England's sternest examination yet.

France.text: Ousmane Dembélé dismantled Norway almost single-handedly, scoring three times inside 32 minutes to render the contest academic before Doué added a fourth in stoppage time — a statement performance that announced France as genuine title contenders. Sweden arrive in the Round of 32 on Tue Jun 30 14:00 PT as a manageable assignment for a side playing with this kind of authority; the Swedes are capable of an upset but France, in this form, start as heavy favourites. Should they advance through Paraguay in the Round of 16, a quarterfinal on Thu Jul 9 13:00 PT against either Canada or Morocco could provide a sterner examination — and a potential semifinal against Spain, Portugal, or Belgium would test whether this team's ambitions are as serious as Dembélé's hat-trick suggested.

Germany.text: Germany's tournament ends in familiar, painful fashion. They trailed to Enciso's first-half strike, levelled through Havertz after the break, and then surrendered to the shootout — a 3-4 defeat on penalties that felt grimly inevitable once extra time arrived. A group-stage finish of ten goals scored and six points suggested a side with real attacking momentum, yet Paraguay exposed the fragility lurking beneath it, and Germany are out in the Round of 32, bundled out by a team ranked far beneath them on reputation alone.

Ghana.text: Ghana scraped through the group stage on merit rather than momentum, finishing third after Croatia came from ahead to win 2-1 — Vlasic's late goal punishing a Ghana side that had briefly levelled through Luckassen just after the hour. They face Colombia in the Round of 32 on Fri Jul 3 18:30 PT, a tie that favors the South Americans on reputation and current standing; Ghana will need a sharper, more disciplined performance to cause an upset. Survive that, and Algeria or Switzerland await in the Round of 16, before a potential quarterfinal collision with Argentina looms as the draw's most daunting possibility.

Haiti.text: Haiti's final group game against Morocco ended 4-2, a scoreline that flatters neither side in quite the right way — the Haitians led briefly, equalized before half-time through Isidor, yet conceded four times across a match Morocco ultimately controlled with growing authority. Two late Moroccan goals in the final stages confirmed what the second half had suggested: Haiti were outclassed and outrun. They finish bottom of Group C without a point, their tournament defined by an 8-2 aggregate against them across three matches, and exit a World Cup that exposed the gulf between their ambition and the level required.

Iran.text: Iran's group stage ends with a 1-1 draw against Egypt — a match in which they surrendered the initiative inside five minutes before Rezaeian levelled early in the second quarter, and neither side could force a winner. Three draws across three games told the full story of a tournament in which Iran were never beaten but never convincing, accumulating just enough to squeeze through as one of the best third-placed sides. They exit at the round of 32, undone not by any single defeat but by a persistent inability to turn resistance into results.

Iraq.text: Iraq's group stage ended in humiliation against Senegal, a 5-0 thrashing that was effectively settled inside the first minute and ruthlessly extended across both halves. The Africans scored through five different players, the goals arriving in waves rather than any sustained Iraqi resistance. It was the punctuation mark on a tournament Iraq would sooner forget — three defeats, twelve goals conceded, none scored, and elimination from a competition they technically advanced into as a best third-placed side, only to find the step up in class absolute and unforgiving.

Ivory Coast.text:
Wait — the brief shows Ivory Coast's last match as a loss in the Round of 32, and `eliminated` is false, but there's no `road` array provided. This is contradictory: a loss in a knockout round should mean elimination, yet `eliminated` is marked false.

Before I write the blurb, could you clarify:

1. **Should `eliminated` be `true`?** If so, I'll treat this as an exit and write the retrospective version.
2. **Is there a `road` array missing from the brief?** Perhaps the Round of 32 result was intended to be a group-stage match, and Ivory Coast actually progressed.

I want to make sure the blurb is factually accurate to your data rather than papering over the inconsistency.

Japan.text: Japan held Brazil until the hour mark, Sano's 29th-minute opener giving them genuine belief before Casemiro levelled and Martinelli's stoppage-time winner completed a cruel late twist. That is how their tournament ends — not in a collapse but in heartbreak, undone in the final seconds of a contest they had controlled for long enough to dare to dream. Japan exit the 2026 World Cup with heads held high, their courage and organisation evident throughout a group stage that brought five points and seven goals, but the knockout rounds proved one step too far.

Jordan.text:
Jordan's final group-stage outing against Argentina followed a familiar script: two goals down by the half-hour mark, Al-Tamari pulling one back just after the interval to briefly threaten a comeback, before Messi settled the contest with ten minutes to go. It was a respectable showing in impossible company, but the gulf in class was never seriously in doubt.

Winless across three matches and with a goal difference of minus five, Jordan nonetheless advanced as one of the best third-placed sides — only to exit at that stage, their tournament concluding without a knockout fixture to call their own.

Mexico.text: Mexico concluded their group campaign in emphatic fashion, sweeping aside Czech Republic 3-0 with a professional, controlled performance — the scoring came in a concentrated burst either side of the hour mark, and a stoppage-time third put a definitive gloss on a clean sheet that confirmed top spot in Group A with a perfect nine points. Ecuador await in the Round of 32 on Tue Jun 30 18:00 PT, a winnable but hardly trivial assignment against a South American side capable of disruption. Survive that, and England loom as the most daunting name in the Round of 16 pool, while a potential quarterfinal collision with Brazil would test just how far this unbeaten Mexico side can genuinely go.

Morocco.text: Morocco scraped through the Round of 32 in the most dramatic fashion imaginable — Diop's stoppage-time equaliser cancelling out Gakpo's opener for the Netherlands before the Atlas Lions held their nerve to win 3-2 on penalties, a result that flattered their resilience as much as their quality. Canada await in the Round of 16 on Sat Jul 4 10:00 PT, a winnable assignment on paper against a side still finding its feet at this level, though Morocco will need to show considerably more in open play than they managed against the Dutch. Survive that, and France, Paraguay, or Sweden could provide a genuine examination in the quarterfinal on Thu Jul 9 13:00 PT — with France representing a potentially seismic collision between two nations with unfinished business.

Netherlands.text: There's a cruel symmetry to how the Netherlands exited: Gakpo's 72nd-minute strike looked to have settled a tense Round of 32 tie against Morocco, only for Diop to equalise in stoppage time and drag it to a shootout the Dutch lost 2-3. A tournament in which they topped Group F with seven points and a +10 goal difference ended not through any tactical failing but through the lottery of penalties, a brutal conclusion for a side that had looked one of the more convincing teams in the competition.

New Zealand.text: Belgium dismantled New Zealand with something close to contempt, Trossard scoring either side of half-time before De Bruyne and Lukaku twisted the knife — the All Whites' consolation on 84 minutes a cosmetic gesture against a 5-1 final score. New Zealand's tournament is over: they finish fourth in Group G, their single point earned somewhere earlier in the group stage, the gap in class against Europe's elite ultimately impossible to bridge.

Norway.text: Norway squeezed past Ivory Coast in a match that turned twice: Nusa's first-half opener gave them control, Diallo's equaliser on 74 minutes introduced genuine anxiety, before Haaland settled nerves with a late winner four minutes from time. The reward is a Round of 16 date with Brazil on Sun Jul 5 13:00 PT — a formidable proposition by any measure, and one in which Norway enter as clear underdogs against a side of considerably greater pedigree and depth, though Haaland's late-game ruthlessness is the kind of variable that keeps such ties open. Survive that, and England or Argentina lurk as possibilities further down the road.

Panama.text: Panama's group stage exit was confirmed with a 0-2 defeat to England, a result that flattered neither side in the opening hour before Bellingham and Kane struck in quick succession midway through the second half to put the tie beyond doubt. Three losses, no points, and a four-goal deficit across Group L tells the full story of a tournament in which Panama never found their footing at this level. They depart having shown that the step up to a 48-team World Cup brought them no easier path to the knockout rounds.

Paraguay.text: Paraguay held Germany to 1-1 through ninety minutes — Enciso's first-half strike cancelled by Havertz shortly after the break — before edging the shootout 4-3 in one of the tournament's genuine upsets. The Round of 16 on Sat Jul 4 14:00 PT brings either France or Sweden, and whichever emerges, Paraguay enter as clear underdogs: France would represent a formidable test of elite quality, Sweden a stern but more navigable proposition. Survive that and Canada or Morocco await in the quarterfinal, with the semifinal potentially producing a collision with Spain, Portugal, or the United States.

Portugal.text: A goalless draw with Colombia to close the group stage — tidy enough defensively, if hardly a statement of intent — sees Portugal advance as runners-up from Group K. They face Croatia in the Round of 32 on Thu Jul 2 16:00 PT, a fixture that looks more demanding on paper than routine: Zlatko Dalić's side have a proven knockout pedigree, and Portugal's muted finishing in recent matches means this is no formality, even if the Selecção's overall quality makes them narrow favourites. Survive that, and Spain could await in the Round of 16 — a genuine heavyweight collision — with France lurking as a potential semifinal opponent should Portugal's path through the quarterfinal hold.

Qatar.text: Qatar's group stage exit arrived with little ceremony, a 1-3 defeat to Bosnia & Herzegovina that laid bare the limitations of a side that rarely threatened to trouble the tournament's upper tier. Behind by two goals before half-time following strikes from Alajbegovic and Abunada, Al-Haydos pulled one back on the stroke of the interval to offer a fleeting flicker of hope, only for Mahmic to extinguish it in the 80th minute. The hosts of 2022 finished bottom of Group B and depart with a single point to their name, a tournament record that reflects, honestly enough, where this squad currently stands among the world's best.

Saudi Arabia.text: Saudi Arabia's group stage ended with a goalless draw against Cape Verde that said little for either side — a flat, scoreless contest that confirmed the Green Falcons' limitations rather than dispelling them. Across three matches they managed a single goal, earned two draws, and finished fourth in Group H, advancing only by the grace of the best third-placed team standings. Their tournament ended there: not in a blaze of controversy or misfortune, but in the quiet arithmetic of a side that rarely threatened and ultimately ran out of road.

Scotland.text: Brazil ended Scotland's tournament with something close to contempt — Júnior struck twice before half-time, the second a stoppage-time flourish that summarised the gulf in class, and Cunha added a third after the interval to make it a thorough, joyless afternoon. Scotland had squeezed through as one of the better third-placed sides, a minor achievement rendered hollow by the manner of their exit. They leave the 2026 World Cup having won once in three matches, outscored four goals to one, and ultimately exposed by the first genuine elite opposition they faced.

Senegal.text: Senegal closed their group campaign with a statement: a 5-0 demolition of Iraq that flattered no one, with the goals spread across both halves and Gueye alone registering twice inside fifteen second-half minutes. They scraped through as one of the best third-placed teams, but that ruthlessness against Iraq at least demonstrates there is firepower in this side. Belgium await in the Round of 32 on Wed Jul 1 13:00 PT — a genuine test, with the Red Devils carrying considerably more pedigree and tournament expectation, though Senegal's attacking punch means this is no foregone conclusion. Survive that, and Portugal or Spain could be lurking in the quarterfinal.

South Africa.text: A stoppage-time strike from Eustáquio condemned South Africa to a 1-0 defeat by Canada in the Round of 32 — a cruel ending to a match they may well feel they deserved more from, edged out only by a goal that arrived when the draw seemed the likeliest outcome. Their tournament is over, then, not with a collapse but with a wound: eliminated in the last breath of normal time, denied a place in the last sixteen by the narrowest and most punishing of margins.

South Korea.text: South Korea's group stage ended with a 0-1 defeat to South Africa, Maseko's 63rd-minute strike enough to send the Taeguk Warriors out on the wrong note — a flat, uninspiring exit that offered little in mitigation. They had scrapped through as one of the best third-placed sides, but the tournament caught up with them in Atlanta, and a side that never quite found cohesion or cutting edge departed at the first hurdle, their World Cup lasting exactly three matches and yielding just three points.

Spain.text: Spain closed out their group with a controlled, if unspectacular, 1-0 win over Uruguay — Baena's first-half strike the only goal of an absorbing contest that La Roja shaded without ever needing second gear. They meet Austria in the Round of 32 on Thu Jul 2 12:00 PT, a tie that reads comfortably in Spain's favour: Austria are a capable European side, but a Spanish team that has conceded nothing across three games carries both the quality and the momentum to advance without serious alarm. Beyond that, the draw grows considerably thornier — Croatia or Portugal likely await in the Round of 16, a potentially defining European rivalry before France or Morocco could loom in the semifinals.

Sweden.text: Sweden scraped through the group stage on goal difference as one of the best third-placed sides, their final group game against Japan a nervy, reactive affair — Maeda's 56th-minute opener forcing Elanga to equalise seven minutes later rather than Sweden dictating terms. The reward for that modest passage is an immediate collision with France in the Round of 32 on Tue Jun 30 14:00 PT, as daunting a first knockout assignment as the draw could have produced; Les Bleus arrive as heavy favourites, and Sweden will need to be considerably more authoritative than they were against Japan to have any hope of an upset. Survive that, and Paraguay await in the Round of 16, before a potential quarterfinal against Canada or Morocco opens a corridor that, in optimistic projection, could eventually lead to a semifinal against the likes of Spain or Portugal.

Switzerland.text: Switzerland topped Group B with a controlled performance against Canada, taking the lead through Vargas seconds into the second half and doubling it through Manzambi before a late David goal made it uncomfortable — a 2-1 win that flattered the Canadians slightly but confirmed Swiss composure when it mattered. They meet Algeria in the Round of 32 on Thu Jul 2 20:00 PT, a tie Switzerland enter as clear favourites; Algeria are capable of disruption but the Swiss, organised and difficult to break down, should advance without alarm. Beyond that, Colombia or Ghana await in the Round of 16, before a potential quarterfinal reckoning with Argentina — the kind of test that would define their tournament entirely.

Tunisia.text:
There are a few data issues worth flagging before writing this blurb.

The `status` field says Tunisia "advanced as a best third-placed team out of Group F," but `finish` says "4th place," `eliminated` is true, and the record shows 0 points from three losses — a team that cannot have advanced. The `last_match` also has an apparent error: "Skhiri 3'" and "Hecke 62'" appear in `opponent_goals` (Netherlands), but Skhiri and Hecke are not Dutch players — Skhiri is Tunisian, and Hecke is associated with Belgian/Dutch club football but the own-goal or misattribution here is unclear. Most strikingly, one of Tunisia's listed `opponent_goals` is likely a Tunisian own goal or a data-entry mix-up.

1. Did Tunisia finish 4th and exit, or did they genuinely advance?
2. Are the goal attributions correct — specifically, were Skhiri and Hecke own goals credited to Netherlands, or are they misplaced in the wrong scorer list?

Once the brief is consistent, I'll write the blurb accurately.

Turkey.text: Turkey's group stage exit arrived with the cruel drama their tournament deserved — a 3-2 win over the United States that was ultimately rendered meaningless in the standings. The Americans struck first through Trusty inside three minutes, yet Güler and Yilmaz turned things around before half-time, only for Berhalter to level early in the second half; Ayhan's stoppage-time header deep into added time sealed the points but not progress. Turkey finished fourth in Group D, their one victory coming too late and too narrow in goal difference to carry them through, and so a tournament of intermittent promise ends without a knockout stage to show for it.

USA.text: The United States finished top of Group D despite a bruising final group game against Turkey — Trusty's third-minute opener gave way to a Turkish surge, and Ayhan's goal deep into stoppage time turned a 2-2 draw into a 3-2 defeat, a late sting that will have stiffened rather than settled the camp. As group winners, they face Bosnia & Herzegovina in the Round of 32 on Wed Jul 1 17:00 PT, a fixture where the Americans enter as clear favourites, though Bosnia are no side to be taken lightly on the break. Survive that, and Belgium or Senegal await in the Round of 16, before a potential quarterfinal collision with Portugal or Spain would pose the sternest examination of how far this team has genuinely come.

Uruguay.text: Uruguay's group stage ended with a narrow 0-1 defeat to Spain, Baena's first-half strike proving the solitary difference in a contest that felt tighter than the scoreline suggests — a game Uruguay never quite unlocked despite staying in it until the final whistle. They leave the tournament having advanced as one of the best third-placed teams, only to find no road beyond the group: two draws and a single defeat across three matches tell the story of a side that ground out points without ever imposing themselves, their tournament concluding in the group stage of World Cup 2026.

Uzbekistan.text: Uzbekistan signed off from their maiden World Cup with a 1-3 defeat to DR Congo that told a familiar story: an early Shomurodov strike offered fleeting promise, before the Congolese took control with a penalty from Wissa, a Mayele effort, and a late third that confirmed the margin was entirely deserved. They finish bottom of Group K without a point, having conceded eleven goals across three matches — a brutal education at football's highest level. Central Asia's standard-bearers depart having shown courage simply in qualifying, but the gap in class exposed here gives the next generation a clear-eyed reckoning to build from.
